text:
3 Months of Hard Work and Almost No Results Please Help Me Figure This Out!
I’m hoping someone can help me figure out what I might be doing wrong with my weight loss journey because I’m feeling really discouraged.
I’m 27 years old 5’1” female with PCOS. About 3 months ago I weighed 78 kg (172 lbs). I got down to 76 kg (167 lbs), but then my weight went back up to 78 kg and has basically stayed there ever since.
For the last 3 months, I’ve been tracking my calories. I can’t say my tracking is 100% perfect, but I’m usually between 1,500–1,700 calories per day, and most days I’m closer to 1,500. On weekends I occasionally have a cheat meal, but it’s usually just one meal, not a whole cheat day.
My exercise routine:
Weight lifting 4 days per week (about 40 minutes each session) 30 minutes of treadmill walking after lifting. At least 10,000 steps every single day
What’s frustrating is that my measurements are basically the same as when I started, and now that I’m back at 78 kg, it feels like I’ve made no progress at all. I do feel stronger and my lifts have improved significantly, but I don’t see any noticeable body changes.
Has anyone experienced something similar? Is there something obvious I might be missing? Should I lower calories, increase activity, track differently?
I really don’t want to give up, but after 3 months of effort with almost no visible results, I’m starting to feel like I’m going crazy.
Any advice would be appreciated.
label: advice

text:
The stigma around counting calories/tracking
I just need to vent or need to know if you guys also experience this in your everyday life.

I track my calories. Have been tracking them for a few months now. I'm fairly strict about counting in my day to day life, but i skip days sometimes especially when traveling. I eat 2000+ calories/day. Which is probably more than I did before I started tracking.

But whenever I tell people I count my calories (i only tell people when they ask me how i progressed so much in the gym, I lift heavy and my goals are always to lift heavier), or if someone sees me log them, i often get looks and/or comments about having an eating disorder. It deeply annoys me.

I'm not obsessive about it. I even take a few days break here and there to not have the habbit of tracking obsessively and still be able to live everyday life without it and I feel perfectly fine when I do.

My partner keeps telling me that I'm starting to develop an eating disorder and it kind lowkey pisses me off. I just want to get my macros in.

Yesterday I went out, and by the end of the night we went to mcdonald's but decided to track beforehand for what I was gonna get. And my friend told me this was unhealthy behaviour.

I use MacroFacor, which calculates my TDEE very well if I use it tediously. I'm strict about logging, I'm not all that strict about what I eat.

Anyways, i wanted to get it off my chest. Does this happen to you too?
label: discussion

text:
Why is high-intensity cardio so rarely recommended for weight loss on this sub?
Every time someone asks for advice on how to lose weight here, you’ll get a lot of comments à la “start lifting heavy every other day and reduce your calories in”, and cardio either isn’t mentioned at all or just walking. It’s like people here don’t believe that cardio will make you shed fat.

I wonder why that is. I’ve always eaten far more than other girls and women my size and height. I’ve never done a conscious weight-loss diet. All the weight I’ve ever lost is because I thought I was eating enough for my exercise levels, especially cardio, and high lean body mass. Turns out I didn’t eat enough, because I lose weight at 2200 calories a day. And that’s because of exercise, because of weightlifting because it builds lean muscle mass, but because of cardio in particular, because cardio gets that lean muscle mass moving.

Intense cardio simply burns a lot more calories than either weightlifting or walking. An hour of running or swimming will burn more than twice what you burn in an hour of weightlifting at the gym. When I used to swim 160 laps a day, I ate like the growing young men around me, and I still lost weight. Because I was burning 800 calories in the pool every day, and active the rest of the day.

So why is cardio so ignored here? It’s pretty obvious that the best and healthiest thing to do is to focus on both cardio, for the heart and endurance and fat loss, and weightlifting, to build muscle and bone density. And if you do both, you don’t have to control your calories in all that much. I eat a lot, and most of it is super high-calorie (my favourite snacks in between meals are salami and sugared nuts), I don’t eat any fruit and barely any vegetables, and I have a sixpack and can do 15 pull-ups in a set.

I use chronometer and basically tell it to call me “not active” and just count my Apple Watch active calories instead of estimate, but my starting value is like 900kcal per day, with activity it goes to ~1200-1500 depending. I have used cycling as cardio and continue that 2-3x per week and run/walk or row on the days I lift

In 6 weeks I’ve lost 6 pounds and can’t see a difference. Seriously didn’t expect this to be as mentally challenging to stay disciplined as it is. Just. Solidarity too if this is you I guess. You’re definitely not alone /endrant

text:
Anyone else feel like everyone is trying to achieve some “dream body” that is likely unrealistic?
I (5’2, 23F), started checking out this thread to inspire me throughout my own weight loss/weight lifting journey. Throughout, however, I’ve noticed a lot of people are trying to continuously lose weight with the idea that that will finally give them this model hourglass flat stomach physique it seems everyone is after.

Make no mistake, I am all for people loving themselves and their bodies and if you decide losing extra weight and gaining more muscle will help, go for it! But am I alone in wondering if some people need to work less on their exercise and diet regime and more on accepting and loving themselves as they are right now?
Weight loss helped my confidence and self esteem but it wasn’t a cure. The things I didn’t like about my body didn’t go away, I learnt to love them (or at least feel indifferent) along the way. I think it’s important to talk about the fact that weight loss itself won’t magically make you happy with how your body looks.

text:
“Body Positive” Friends
I’m a college student right now and I’ve struggled with my weight my entire life. I’m 5’2 and had been overweight since elementary school until around November when I finally reached a normal weight under BMI standards. My figure has been impacted quite a bit. I love my friends to death but many of them are very avoidant of body talk because they subscribe to body positivity and some of them are plus size. Having once been a plus sized person I think that bigger people deserve to exist without being ridiculed because ultimately they are people and can make their own choices and it’s not right to make assumptions. I also think that fat people absolutely can be beautiful and attractive. However, I personally feel very insecure in a bigger body and a smaller body makes me feel more confident. I feel alone though when I try to express my frustration over weight gain and it’s emotional impact and they become dismissive and quiet about it, but when it’s on the other side of the spectrum they’re so quick to be reassuring (which I think people are totally deserving of but it’s upsetting to not be validated) Has anyone else experienced this?

text:
i will forever stand by that fixing your diet will help you with weight loss and increasing movement will help with weight matience
this could be slightly controversial, but I feel like dieting is more effective than just an increase in movement for weight loss. Yes both of them will aid in it, but I feel like your weight is mostly influenced by your diet. You can’t outrun a shit diet. however, if you are just trying to maintain your weight, adding in a bit more movement can help burn off the extra calories that might be stored as fat and help increase muscle stimulus, which help regulate metabolism. also movement increases insulin sensitivity so you process food better.

yes, a mix of both of them is probably gonna get you optimal results, but I feel like you should adjust one of these things depending on what phase you’re in. When you’re at maintenance, you obviously want to enjoy life a little bit and would be in hell if you had to be living off chicken and broccoli for the rest of your life.

I’ve recently been getting videos of older women who are in their 60’s and 70’s doing easy low impact exercises on the ground to help show women how to do work on their pelvic floor and generally move to be healthy. These women are awesome and you can tell they put in the time to build strength and eat healthy.

The amount of people in the comments has me fuming…they all say but what if I can’t get down on the floor…I wouldn’t be able to get up off the floor….that’s too hard…mind you the people commenting are younger than the lady in the video, some in their 50’s and 40’s even.

That is so freaking sad that they can’t get off the floor. If I’m 50 and can’t get off the floor, a basic movement to survive then I don’t know what I’m doing. *Disabilities aside of course, these appear to be normal people.

Fitness is so important, the movements you’re doing in your 20’s, 30’s, 40’s and 50’s really impact the type of life you’re going to have when you’re older.

Reminder to work on basic stretches and skills early! They will literally mean the difference between life and death when it comes down to it.

That’s all. Sorry for ranting 😅
label: discussion

text:
How are you guys navigating this?
I had to battle a lot of mental challenges for my weight loss journey to finally click. But one thing I can't for the LIFE of me let go is the irritation I get from people that want to "go on the journey" with me. They ask you how you manage and you give them the standard advice "calorie deficit. Walk more. Work on your mental health" but that's not what they want to hear. They want to hear "I just ate eggs all week and lost 50 pounds". Like everything you say is met with a "but I can't do that because". So now we're just going back and forth cause I refuse to tell her an easier route and I'm getting irritated that she's constantly saying what she can't do.

There's legit something so upsetting about working hard on something for someone to complain that they can't do it because of whatever excuse. Like do you think I have time, money, patience for this? No. I'm annoyed. I want pizza. I'd rather go home and sleep. But I HAVE to do this because I love myself and I promised myself that I'd make changes.

This journey is not easy. It's for sure not a temporary thing. We're making life long changes and habits that will keep us healthy. Stop complaining and do the damn work.

How are yall managing the mental load of other people?
label: discussion

text:
Weight fluctuations are such an odd thing

The sudden drop between 197 is so weird and random. I don’t have regular cycles so I don’t have a repetitive natural cycle of weight changes. Though I wish to track that one day if and when I can get my periods to level out naturally.

I started eating better in March for sure - not really calorie counting but trying more vegetables. It is hard with adhd, pcos, insulin resistance, fatty liver, and prediabetes but I know my body will thank me by giving me better sleep, better stamina and eventually an improved liver.

At this point I don’t know if I say I’ve lost 5 lbs or if I’ve lost 9, but either way i like how comfier my jeans fit at the moment. A tiny bit looser around the waist since I’m apple shaped.

I used a habit tracking app to remember to check the scale every morning (or whenever I wake up,) and that’s helped vs randomly stepping on the scale at odd hours.

I know with some people the scale can be very triggering and there are many other ways to track progress - eg with how your pants fit or how you feel in your joints. But for me personally having visual data keeps me more motivated especially with adhd. Object permanence is so real 😭

text:
Don’t be afraid to bulk!
I’ve been seeing a lot on this sub that people want to either “recomp” or cut. Ladies, gals, and non binary pals, PLEASE don’t be afraid to bulk! This obviously depends on person to person but “bulk” has gotten such a bad reputation among the femme fitness community.

If you’re telling people you want to see muscles you have to build said muscles, and that comes from eating in a caloric surplus. Also if you’re new to the gym you probably want to see those sweet sweet gains. And the best way to get those gains? Bulking. If you’re more advanced in the gym I would recommend a recomp or even a cut. But you need to build that base if you want that booty shape.

text:
Burning out.
I’m just so frustrated and why does this have to be so hard and mentally draining?!

I recently hired a personal trainer and I feel like I’m burning out.

So I usually do 3 workouts a week, zone 2 run/boxing/sprints. So it’s 3 days of working out. And I really really enjoyed it.

Decided to get more serious into weight training because I wanna build some muscle and the ladies in my family all have osteoporosis with knees and hip replacements so I thought that I probably should lift and my PT tacked on a 3 day lifting session a week.

Obviously I can’t do all that in a week! It’ll be 6 workout sessions! I tried and in less than a month I’m just like a flickering candle. Just tired and constantly sore and grumpy. I refuse to stop my cardio sessions because it’s a stress reliever for me and helps my mental health. But I’m so exhausted by the weights that sometimes I miss out on my cardio and this has led to me being upset (because I usually run/sprint my frustrations away).

My PT doesn’t believe in cardio and says I have to prioritize weight training?? I’ve spoken to him and he’s very adamant that I need to train 3 times a week for results.

He says superset also helps cardio. I call bullshit. We have “argued” over this a few times. My cardio sessions have not been productive because I’m sore and tired and my vo2 max have been taking a hit.

Just wanted to scream this out into the reddit void. Thanks for reading.

text:
Strength standards for petite lifters (why bodyweight ratios are misleading)
5'1", 105lbs. Deadlift 205, squat 155, bench 95.

People always say "wow that's almost 2x bodyweight on deadlift!" Yeah but I'm still only moving 205 pounds which is objectively not that heavy.

The bodyweight ratio problem: makes petite lifters' numbers sound impressive when they're still moving light absolute weight. A 150lb person deadlifting 300 is moving way more actual weight than me at "almost 2x bodyweight."

Physics doesn't scale linearly with body size. Smaller people have mechanical advantages on bodyweight ratios but that doesn't mean we're proportionally stronger.

The flip side: equipment doesn't scale to petite bodies. Barbell is too long. Plates are huge relative to my frame. Some machines don't adjust small enough.

Training is harder because equipment doesn't fit, but results sound more impressive because of ratio math.

Anyone else frustrated by the "impressive for your size" comments?

text:
6 months progress
I don’t really have anywhere to show progress, so here I am. The first photo was around September 2025 and I didn’t start a calorie deficit and working out til January 2026. I’ve only do 10k step five days a week, and I workout at home with weights anywhere from 3-5 day a week. I’m kinda unorthodox with this but I just try to be consistent with my calorie deficit. So I started low carb like 50-70g total carbs and 1400-1500 calories but now I’m doing 1350 calories, 50g net carbs and 120-130g protein. I was 225 and now I’m 197 lbs and I’m 5’2.5. Prior to working out a doctor said I was pre-diabetic, and I do have PCOS/PMOS, so I average about 5-6lbs weight loss a month. On the weekends I kinda just don’t count but I stay away from carbs except fruits and veggies or sometimes I count everything, lol the weekends I get super lazy with it but my overall first goal is 160lbs, this is the first time I’ve ever stuck to losing weight and it’s very satisfying. I was able to get out the pre-diabetic range, and that was stressing me out the most lol.

but your consistency and discipline is gonna take you far. You got this.

text:
1 year results

I’m a couple days late bc ~life~ but here are my 1 year results of weight training & calorie counting. Official date is July 1st. I started this journey after already losing 22LBS from simply lessening my portion sizes. I made little swaps like more water and less juice/soda, parking a little farther from the store instead of finding a super close parking spot and trying to incorporate more veggies into my diet. I was 213lbs when I began on July 1st, 2024. I am now 132lbs. I get a lot of questions in my DMs regarding loose skin so I’ve added the last two photos to show that it’s particularly bad on my arms and stomach the most. I would say throughout this year my key takeaways were following a whole food diet 80% of the time and “fun” foods 20% of the time, weight training, getting between 7K-10K steps a day minimum, drinking a lot of water and prioritizing rest/sleep.

Getting started advice
Hi all!

I'm a new sub looking for advice and guidance. I'm currently 28 years old, 5'0, and weigh 158 lbs. I have just started my fitness journey - I started walking 2.5 miles a day (3-4x a week) and have been trying intermittent fasting since January. To get serious and start, I'm thinking of hitting 8-10k steps a day and trying out Caroline Girvan's Epic 1 series.

However, I struggle to focus on my nutrition (I eat out more than I'd like, and I get bored with my meals quickly). I'm assuming counting my calories and intake is what I should move towards next instead of fasting.

text:
Struggling to Lose Weight - Seeking Advice and Guidance
Hey girls,

I'm 25F, 88kg, and being super honest, I've noticed that my body gains fat very easily. I really have to be more conscious than others when it comes to my diet and lifestyle.

I've been working out for about two months now. I started at around 91kg, and for the past month, my weight has been stuck at 88kg. There's been no significant weight loss, despite eating clean. I do slip up sometimes, like having bread, roti, or poori once or twice a week, but no major junk food.

My routine is pretty hectic — I have an 8-hour job with a 3-hour commute, followed by the gym, and then straight to bed. I mostly rely on my office for meals, which includes healthy options like salads and balanced plates, and I strictly monitor my intake.

Despite this, I’m not seeing the results I hoped for, and it’s starting to feel a bit discouraging.

I also often feel a bit dumb in the gym, constantly running to my trainer to ask what to do next. I know there's nothing wrong with that — I pay for the guidance, after all — but I’d love to feel a bit more independent and confident about my workouts.

So, my question is, how did you guys gain the knowledge and confidence to work out effectively? Is there a step-by-step guide for this, like what order to follow for different muscle groups? For example, on leg day, should it be squats first, then curls, then leg press, then calf raises? Does changing the sequence impact results, or is there a proper way to structure a workout?

Would really appreciate any advice or tips. Thanks in advance!

text:
[Advice and Encouragement Needed] Getting back into exercise and developing a healthy relationship with it!
I used to workout and do circuits quite regularly last year; almost had abs which has been my goal for ages!!

Lockdown and stress and mental health happened and I basically just stopped exercising sans walking. I could do 10-20 good pushups easily last time, and now I can barely do 5.

I’ve lost weight sure, but it’s all my arm and butt muscles disappearing. It’s just so discouraging comparing my current self to my previous self.

Any advice on how to get back on it? Would love to hear some of your experiences!!
